incidents.victim: University of Mississippi Medical Center (UMMC) & Passaic County, New Jersey
incidents.vector: Exploitation of a public-facing application, followed by lateral movement, credential theft, and deployment of ransomware that encrypted the EHR platform and communications infrastructure.
incidents.impact: UMMC's 35 clinics were forced offline for nine days (Feb 19–Mar 2), disrupting cancer infusions, trauma care, and elective procedures at Mississippi's only Level I trauma center; attackers demanded an $800,000 ransom and threatened to leak stolen patient data by March 20. Passaic County, NJ simultaneously had phone lines and IT systems taken down by the same group, which also demanded $800,000.

incidents.victim: TELUS Digital (BPO and digital services arm of Telus Corporation)
incidents.vector: Attackers leveraged Google Cloud Platform credentials stolen during an earlier supply-chain breach of the Salesloft Drift platform in 2025, then moved laterally through Telus Digital's cloud environment — including a large BigQuery instance — to exfiltrate data at scale.
incidents.impact: Criminals claimed theft of approximately 1 petabyte of data, including BPO customer PII, FBI background checks, source code, financial records, call-center voice recordings, and Salesforce data for dozens of enterprise customers; the company rejected a reported $65 million ransom demand and the investigation into affected customers remains ongoing.

incidents.vector: Attackers compromised Stryker's Microsoft Intune mobile device management environment and weaponised its native device-wipe capability to remotely erase more than 200,000 servers, mobile devices, and endpoints across 79 countries — no traditional ransomware or malware binary was deployed.
incidents.impact: Order processing, manufacturing, and shipping operations were severely disrupted; Stryker filed an SEC 8-K disclosing the incident and stated a full restoration timeline was unknown; approximately 50 TB of critical data was reportedly destroyed, raising supply-chain concerns for healthcare providers globally.

incidents.victim: Wisconsin ambulance services provider (largest in state, unnamed)
incidents.vector: Details of the initial intrusion vector have not been publicly disclosed; the attack compromised systems used to manage patient and operational data.
incidents.impact: Over 235,000 individuals were confirmed affected by the cyberattack on the state's largest ambulance provider; incident details and the full scope of data exposure remain under investigation.

incidents.victim: Ivanti Endpoint Manager, SolarWinds Web Help Desk, VMware Aria Operations (enterprise infrastructure — multiple victim organisations globally)
incidents.attacker: Unknown (multiple threat actors — nation-state and criminal involvement suspected)
incidents.vector: Active exploitation of three critical vulnerabilities: an authentication bypass in Ivanti EPM (CVE-2026-1603, CVSS 8.6), a remote-code-execution deserialization flaw in SolarWinds Web Help Desk (CVE-2025-26399, CVSS 9.8), and a command-injection flaw in VMware Aria Operations (CVE-2026-22719, CVSS 8.1); CISA added all three to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and issued emergency patch deadlines.
incidents.impact: Federal civilian agencies were ordered to remediate by March 23–24, 2026; exploitation was confirmed in the wild across enterprise and government networks globally, with VMware Aria's broad visibility into server, network, and cloud infrastructure making it a particularly high-value target for persistent access.

incidents.victim: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN users (government and critical infrastructure globally, including EU European Commission, Dutch and Finnish governments)
incidents.attacker: Unknown (nation-state involvement assessed by Five Eyes agencies)
incidents.vector: Exploitation of authentication bypass and path-traversal vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-20127, CVE-2022-20775) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager products, enabling unauthenticated remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges and establish long-term persistence, including root access and disabled logging.
incidents.impact: CISA issued Emergency Directive 26-03; UK NCSC and multiple Five Eyes allies simultaneously warned that global organisations were compromised; attackers established persistent footholds in government and private-sector networks, with the NCSC urging organisations to actively hunt for signs of compromise.

incidents.victim: Ukraine — energy grid and critical infrastructure
incidents.attacker: Russia — GRU military intelligence (state cyber units)
incidents.vector: Persistent intrusions into energy infrastructure networks, with attackers focused on mapping facilities and tracking repair activities to provide targeting intelligence for kinetic missile and drone strikes rather than immediately causing disruption.
incidents.impact: Ukrainian cybersecurity officials confirmed that Russian cyber operations are now primarily intelligence-collection missions inside energy networks — monitoring damage from physical strikes, tracking recovery progress, and identifying where replacement components are sourced — representing a strategic shift that compounds the physical bombardment campaign.

incidents.vector: Spear-phishing campaigns using judicial and charity-themed lures to deploy a JavaScript-based backdoor (DRILLAPP) running through the Microsoft Edge browser, capable of file upload/download, microphone access, and webcam capture; campaign overlaps with prior GRU-linked operations against Ukrainian defence forces.
incidents.impact: The campaign, active from at least early February 2026, enabled ongoing covert surveillance of Ukrainian military and government personnel; two campaign variants were identified, with the malware providing persistent remote access to targeted endpoints.

incidents.victim: Tycoon 2FA phishing-as-a-service platform (law enforcement action — downstream victims: ~100,000 organisations globally)
incidents.industry: Cross-sector (MFA bypass infrastructure targeting all verticals)
incidents.vector: Tycoon 2FA operated as an adversary-in-the-middle platform sold via Telegram since 2023; it harvested Microsoft 365 and Gmail session cookies through a transparent proxy, bypassing MFA entirely to achieve full account takeover at scale — with over 3 million malicious messages observed in February 2026 alone.
incidents.impact: A coordinated public-private takedown by Proofpoint, Microsoft, Europol, Cloudflare, and international law enforcement seized 330 Tycoon 2FA control-panel domains; the platform had provided criminal access to approximately 100,000 organisations including schools, hospitals, and government bodies — disruption is significant but replication risk remains high given the RaaS proliferation trend.

incidents.vector: A threat actor gained unauthorised access to a limited number of servers hosting legacy data, subsequently exfiltrating approximately 2 GB of records and advertising the stolen data on a cybercriminal forum.
incidents.impact: Exposed data included customer names, user IDs, business contact information (including .gov email addresses), account records for government agencies and law firms, passwords, IT incident tickets, and customer survey data; LexisNexis engaged external forensic experts and notified affected current and former customers across its 150-country footprint.
